pending_diagnosis: DIAGNOSIS: Late-onset Ornithine Transcarbamylase (OTC) Deficiency (a Urea Cycle Disorder) THE SMOKING GUN: The critical clue required advanced specialized testing. The patient’s plasma amino acid profile revealed a high-level Ammonia spike (which eventually peaked at 1200 µmol/L) paired with extremely low plasma Citrulline and Arginine, alongside grossly elevated urinary Orotic Acid. This specific biochemical fingerprint isolates the defect to the beginning of the urea cycle, proximal to the Citrulline synthesis step (the OTC enzyme). The trigger in this adult patient was the high-protein keto diet, which dramatically increased the nitrogenous load, overwhelming the patient's residual enzyme function. PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: Ornithine Transcarbamylase (OTC) is a mitochondrial enzyme necessary for the second step of the urea cycle, which converts ammonia (a toxic byproduct of protein catabolism) into urea for excretion. While typically catastrophic in infancy, partial enzyme function can allow patients to reach adulthood asymptomatic (late-onset OTC deficiency). When the body is subjected to a catabolic stressor (like infection, surgery, or, in this case, a high-protein diet), the production of ammonia exceeds the body's limited capacity to detoxify it. The resultant hyperammonemia leads directly to severe cerebral edema, glial swelling, and ultimately, herniation and death if not immediately managed with aggressive ammonia scavenging (dialysis, ammonia binders).
